elements.sent-2411-001: Trustworthy digital communication requires the secure exchange of contact information, but current approaches lack usability and scalability for larger groups of users.
elements.sent-2411-002: We evaluate the usability of two secure contact exchange systems: the current state of the art, SafeSlinger, and our newly designed protocol, \OurApproach, which extends trust from physical encounters to spontaneous online communication.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our lab study ($N=45$) demonstrates \OurApproach's superior usability, automating the tedious verification tasks from previous approaches via an acoustic out-of-band channel.
elements.sent-2411-004: Although participants significantly preferred our system, minimizing user effort surprisingly decreased the perceived security for some users, who associated security with complexity.
elements.sent-2411-005: We discuss user perceptions of the different protocol components and identify remaining usability barriers for CSCW application scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-006: They are exchanging contact information using the PairSonic protocol, depicted by lines connecting the phones, symbolizing data transmission.
elements.sent-2411-007: The environment suggests an informal, collaborative setting.
elements.sent-2411-008: This figure emphasizes the group dynamic and interaction enabled by PairSonic.. \end{teaserfigure.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Imagine a group of seven researchers who just met for the first time at a conference.
elements.sent-2411-010: During the event, they notice that they share a lot of common interests -- they might even have some ideas for future collaborations -- so they decide to stay in contact.
elements.sent-2411-011: They all have a smartphone, but to securely communicate online, they need a way to securely exchange their contact information.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this paper, we study group pairing methods for the fast and secure exchange of contact information that fulfill the following two goals: first, they allow the participants to contact each other online, to establish new conversations or to start new digital collaborations.
elements.sent-2411-013: Second, they authenticate the participants by leveraging their physical encounter to verify their contact information.\footnote{The exchanged contact information usually contains additional metadata and public-key material, e.g., for secure \gls{E2EE instant messaging.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: There are countless similar use cases for online collaboration besides the aforementioned group of researchers, ranging from professional activities (team communication, remote work, project management), to educational settings (interactive learning platforms, knowledge management), and leisure (event planning and coordination), affecting billions of users in today's digital world \cite{studer2008Survey,kindberg2002System,lundgren2015Designing,handel2002What,namara2021differential .}
elements.sent-2411-015: For all these scenarios, secure exchange of contact information is required to associate known physical individuals with their online profiles and protect their privacy and security when interacting online.
elements.sent-2411-016: Without such a secure contact exchange, an adversary can eavesdrop on private communication or even impersonate one of the communication participants in order to modify or create fake messages that appear legitimate.
elements.sent-2411-017: Effective communication and collaboration thrives in a trusted environment, where participants have clear knowledge of the identities of others involved and no concerns about unauthorized access to their conversations.
elements.sent-2411-018: Such an environment encourages open discussions, even on sensitive topics \cite{shusas2023accounting .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Previous research has shown that users desire authenticated conversations -- wanting to know exactly who they are communicating with -- when talking to friends and family members \cite{fassl2023Why , but also when communicating with colleagues or business partners \cite{fassl2021Exploring , and especially so for vulnerable groups such as journalists and activists \cite{herzberg2021Secure .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Secure contact exchange is therefore an important prerequisite for fruitful and trustworthy online communication and collaboration \cite{oesch2022User .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Throughout the last decade, users have increasingly adopted \acrfull{E2EE tools such as Signal \cite{signalMessenger , Whatsapp \cite{whatsappMessenger , Telegram \cite{telegramMessenger , and Threema \cite{threemaMessenger , facilitating group collaboration with features like real-time messaging, file sharing, and video calls.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: These \gls{E2EE tools also offer the verification of contact information through an authentication ceremony, which requires users to perform a manual verification action, either by scanning \gls{QR codes or by manually comparing public key fingerprints on their devices.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: This authentication ceremony allows users to transfer an existing trust relationship from a physical encounter to the corresponding digital identity of their communication partners, enabling authenticated conversations.
elements.sent-2411-024: There are two problems with the design of authentication ceremonies in state-of-the-art tools for secure communication and collaboration:.
elements.sent-2411-025: Current authentication ceremonies are hard to use, even for professionals.
elements.sent-2411-026: Current authentication ceremonies do not support larger groups of users.
elements.sent-2411-027: First, previous usability research has shown that users struggle to understand and perform authentication ceremonies \cite{oesch2022User,vaziripour2017that,vaziripour2018Action,wu2019Something .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Most users expect that \gls{E2EE tools like Signal are secure by default without further verification actions, when in reality these tools cannot protect against active \gls{MitM attacks and rogue service operators unless the users diligently perform the authentication ceremony \cite{herzberg2021Secure,vasile2020ghost .}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: These authentication ceremonies, however, require considerable user interaction \cite{herzberg2016Can , take a long time \cite{vaziripour2017that , and are difficult to use even for security professionals \cite{schroder2016When,fassl2023Why .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Recently, the risks of active \gls{MitM attacks in \gls{E2EE tools have gained more attention due to reports of law enforcement agencies compromising app providers \cite{levy2018principles,goodin2018police , demonstrating a practical and concerning risk for privacy infringements, especially if such vulnerabilities were to be exploited by malicious actors in the future.}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Second, state-of-the-art tools like Signal and WhatsApp do not support efficient and secure contact exchange for groups of multiple users, but only between two users.
elements.sent-2411-032: This is a substantial shortcoming, as collaboration often involves more than two participants.
elements.sent-2411-033: While each pair of group members could separately perform the bilateral authentication ceremony one after another, this is infeasible as the number of manual verification actions scales quadratically ($\frac{n(n-1) {2}$) with the group size ($n$).}
elements.sent-2411-034: To illustrate this: if our exemplary group of seven researchers were to use Signal, they would need to perform 21 manual actions amongst each other for authentication, but each user can only perform one of these actions at a time.
elements.sent-2411-035: Such an authentication takes too much time and would discourage the spontaneous decision to collaborate.
elements.sent-2411-036: The main challenge is how to authenticate the exchanged contact information and key material without having to rely on any trusted third parties or \glspl{PKI that facilitate the exchange.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Farb et al.~\cite{farb2013SafeSlinger achieved a breakthrough towards solving this problem when they proposed the more usable SafeSlinger protocol for the secure exchange of contact information in the absence of trusted third parties.}
elements.sent-2411-038: While SafeSlinger offers significantly higher usability than alternatives~\cite{farb2013SafeSlinger , it still requires the participants to perform $\frac{n(n-1) {2}$ manual text comparisons to achieve its security guarantees.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Since the release of SafeSlinger, several studies on authentication methods suggested that users generally prefer simpler schemes requiring less involvement \cite{xu2021Key,mehrabi2019Evaluating and that involving users for manual comparisons is prone to errors \cite{kainda2009Usability,tan2017Can,vaziripour2017that .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Furthermore, recent research has explored the intuitive actions users might take to associate multiple devices \cite{chong2013How,jokela2015Connecting , suggesting that the user experience in SafeSlinger may not align with user expectations.}
elements.sent-2411-041: The required amount of user interaction is potentially discouraging, especially considering larger groups.
elements.sent-2411-042: Instead of facilitating trustworthy communication and collaboration, state-of-the-art methods rather inhibit it (see \autoref{sec:related-work for a more extensive discussion of related work).}
elements.sent-2411-043: In this work, we approach the challenge of securely exchanging contact information from a different angle, under the research hypothesis that users would prefer a contact exchange method with minimal user interaction \cite{kuo2008Mind,xu2021Key,mehrabi2019Evaluating .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Consequently, we design a new group pairing protocol named \OurApproach that requires less effort and can efficiently scale to multiple participants.
elements.sent-2411-045: The illustrative group of researchers can exchange their contact information securely and quickly by starting \OurApproach and simply holding their devices close together for a few seconds, aligning with intuitive association behavior \cite{chong2013How,jokela2014FlexiGroups,jokela2015Connecting .}

elements.sent-2411-048: Our main contribution to CSCW is the usability evaluation of our novel acoustic approach \OurApproach for the secure exchange of contact information and cryptographic public keys for groups of multiple users as a basic requirement for teams to work with sensitive content.
elements.sent-2411-049: We conduct a lab study with $N=45$ participants -- consisting of practical authentication tasks on smartphones, subsequent interviews, and a questionnaire -- to compare the state-of-the-art system SafeSlinger with our novel protocol \OurApproach, which we design and implement for smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-050: This paper is structured as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-051: We define the problem and establish our requirements for secure contact exchange (\autoref{sec:problem ), discussing why the state of the art fails to solve this problem (\autoref{sec:related-work ).}}
elements.sent-2411-052: We design \OurApproach, an improved protocol for fast and secure contact exchange, and implement it for smartphones (\autoref{sec:design ).}
elements.sent-2411-053: To answer our research questions (\autoref{sec:research-questions ), aiming to understand how automating the cumbersome verification can improve on the current state of the art (\SafeSlinger), we conduct a lab study with $N=45$ participants to compare both systems (\autoref{sec:methods ).}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Based on the quantitative insights from our questionnaire (\autoref{sec:quantitative ) and qualitative interviews (\autoref{sec:qualitative ), we discuss our observation that less user interaction seems to improve usability in our case, but can have unexpected negative effects on perceived security.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: We evaluate the suitability of the acoustic \gls{OOB channel for pairing and give recommendations how to improve the state of the art of secure contact exchange (\autoref{sec:discussion ).}}
elements.sent-2411-056: We provide a replication package with our evaluation scripts and the pseudonymized dataset from our study, which contains 69 variables for each of the 45 participants \cite{zenodo2024dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-057: This section defines the problem we are addressing (\autoref{sec:problem-definition ) and our assumptions (\autoref{sec:assumptions ).}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Our goal is to let a group of two or more users that physically meet (\autoref{fig:teaser ) spontaneously exchange their contact information \cite{farb2013SafeSlinger .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The expected outcome of this secure contact exchange\footnote{ The process of exchanging and verifying the identities with a group of participants is sometimes also called group key verification, or group authentication ceremony, or peer-to-peer authentication, or secure group contact sharing, or key establishment for groups, or spontaneous device association.}
elements.sent-2411-060: In this work, we call this process secure contact exchange or group pairing, as we always target groups of two or more users unless otherwise noted.
elements.sent-2411-061: We target a group of users who physically meet for the pairing process (\autoref{sec:discussion-meetings ) and want to be able to set up secure communication channels between all subsets of users within the group.}
elements.sent-2411-062: After this pairing process, the users can subsequently communicate in a confidential and authenticated manner over untrusted channels such as the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-063: Our contact exchange protocol has the following requirements: Usability Requirements.
elements.sent-2411-064: Minimal user interaction, due to our research hypothesis that users would prefer such a contact exchange method \cite{kuo2008Mind,xu2021Key,mehrabi2019Evaluating .}
elements.sent-2411-065: The protocol should be easy to use for lay users, facilitating spontaneous ad-hoc collaboration plans \cite{chong2012Usability,chong2014Survey .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Scalable to larger groups of users.
elements.sent-2411-067: The pairing protocol should be fast and not noticeably depend on the number of users.
elements.sent-2411-068: Error-proof, as users can make mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-069: In particular, our protocol should be robust to rushing users who prioritize speed and efficiency over adhering to instructions or prompts, potentially compromising the security of the protocol.
elements.sent-2411-070: Our protocol should seamlessly run on existing \gls{COTS devices, enabling users to leverage its benefits immediately by simply installing our software.}
elements.sent-2411-071: We focus on user-controlled wireless devices such as smartphones, tablets, laptops, and smart watches, without requiring further hardware or firmware modifications.
elements.sent-2411-072: Our protocol should not require any existing security context in the form of pre-shared keys or a jointly trusted third party.
elements.sent-2411-073: Our protocol should operate independently of any central infrastructure and remain fully functional without an active Internet connection.
elements.sent-2411-074: This requirement is crucial to support people's spontaneous decision-making, facilitating seamless collaboration and communication regardless of their location.
elements.sent-2411-075: This means that no metadata can leak to third parties, as part of our privacy-by-design approach.
elements.sent-2411-076: If a security vulnerability arises in our protocol or implementation, the offline nature of our exchange makes exploitation by attackers significantly more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-077: Only the intended participants should receive the contact information.
elements.sent-2411-078: Each received contact information should match the corresponding honest participant.
elements.sent-2411-079: We require mutual authentication, i.e., each participant authenticates all the other participants.
elements.sent-2411-081: This requirement was postulated by Farb et al.~\cite{farb2013SafeSlinger , stipulating that each pair of participants in our groupwise exchange should get the same security properties as if they had performed a direct pairwise exchange.}
elements.sent-2411-082: This implies that adversarial participants cannot degrade the bindings between other honest participants.
elements.sent-2411-083: We assume that all users are physically present at the same location and can utilize the concept of \textquote{group demonstrative identification \cite{li2010Group , which involves legitimate participants excluding unintended participants or imposters based on personal identification, such as appearance or voice.}}

elements.sent-2411-101: Our work focuses on the problem of securely exchanging contact information, including cryptographic key material, in groups of users.
elements.sent-2411-102: This problem relates to several branches of research, which we first summarize and then elaborate in further detail:.

elements.sent-2411-105: Secure device pairing is closely related to our work, as it involves device association aimed at establishing an authentic communication channel \cite{fomichevSurveySystematizationSecure2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Whereas previous work mostly focused on connecting two devices (\textquote{pairing ), our work additionally considers the more complex case of connecting multiple users, although it also functions with just two users.}
elements.sent-2411-107: A key distinction is that we aim not only to establish a secure communication channel for the entire group, but potentially for every subset of group members as well: CSCW research has shown that larger groups sometimes like to split dynamically into smaller task forces for more effective collaboration \cite{harandi2019supporting,umbelino2019prototeams .}

elements.sent-2411-156: It was designed specifically for groups of people to address the security and usability problems of using pairwise protocols in such scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-157: Participants perform the following steps while using SafeSlinger:.
elements.sent-2411-158: Each participant must select the group size.
elements.sent-2411-159: Next, each participant sees a number on their smartphone.
elements.sent-2411-160: They must coordinate to identify and enter the lowest number among all participants on their smartphone.
elements.sent-2411-161: Each participant gets displayed three word phrases.
elements.sent-2411-162: They must coordinate to identify and select the phrase common to all smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-163: The protocol then securely exchanges their contact information.
elements.sent-2411-164: Finally, each participant verifies that they received correct contact information from the others.
elements.sent-2411-165: In their evaluation, SafeSlinger was significantly faster and more usable than the pairwise contact exchange method Bump,\footnote{Bump was discontinued in 2014.}
elements.sent-2411-166: However, each step of the SafeSlinger protocol requires all participants to perform multiple comparisons with all other group members.
elements.sent-2411-167: This increases effort for larger group sizes and is error-prone \cite{kainda2009Usability,tan2017Can .}
elements.sent-2411-168: Furthermore, SafeSlinger requires a stable Internet connection during the pairing process and relies on a third-party server to facilitate the contact exchange.
elements.sent-2411-169: These limitations prevent SafeSlinger from meeting our usability and deployability requirements (\autoref{sec:problem ).}
elements.sent-2411-170: Based on our analysis of related work in the previous section, the main problem of previous approaches is the lack of usability for ordinary users, which gets amplified for larger groups of people due to inadequate scalability.
elements.sent-2411-171: SafeSlinger is the most promising approach so far, but lacks in scalability and deployability.
elements.sent-2411-172: The authentication ceremonies in \gls{E2EE tools, as well as most current secure device pairing methods, are solely designed for two devices.}
elements.sent-2411-173: Users expect a single associating interaction for the entire group \cite{chong2013How,jokela2014FlexiGroups,jokela2015Connecting , but pairwise schemes cannot be easily generalized to multiple participants \cite{farb2013SafeSlinger .}}
elements.sent-2411-174: In this work, we explore the problem of secure contact exchange from a new angle, under the following research hypothesis: users prefer a contact exchange method with minimal user interaction.
elements.sent-2411-175: Consequently, we design a new protocol named \OurApproach that minimizes user interaction, implement it on smartphones, and conduct a lab study comparing it with the current state-of-the-art approach, SafeSlinger.
elements.sent-2411-176: The research questions we address in our work are:.
elements.sent-2411-177: Which initialization and verification steps do users prefer?.
elements.sent-2411-179: How do users perceive the security of both methods?.
elements.sent-2411-180: How do users like the audible acoustic \gls{OOB channel for pairing?.}
elements.sent-2411-181: This section describes the design and implementation of our novel acoustic group pairing protocol, called \OurApproach.
elements.sent-2411-182: We developed it to meet the usability, deployability, and security requirements outlined in \autoref{sec:problem .}
elements.sent-2411-183: Our starting point was the design of SafeSlinger, which we identified as the most promising approach to date in \autoref{sec:related-work , as it fulfills all our security requirements -- but not all usability and deployability requirements.}

The user can select either the "Coordinator" or the "Participant" role. } \label{fig:pairsonic-participant1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.25\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{gfx/ps-participant2.png} \caption { Initialization: Ready. } \Description{ Participants' phones displaying a "ready" status, indicating that they are now waiting for the coordinator. } \label{fig:pairsonic-participant2} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.25\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{gfx/ps-participant3.png} \caption { Verification. } \Description{ Verification phase with phones showing a green checkmark. } \label{fig:pairsonic-participant3} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.25\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{gfx/ps-participant4.png} \caption { Finalization. } \Description{ Final screen displaying exchanged contact details. } \label{fig:pairsonic-participant4} \end{subfigure} \caption { This figure shows the \OurApproach contact exchange from the perspective of the participant role. (a) The participants begin by selecting their role. (b) They then wait for the coordinator to initiate the exchange via the acoustic \gls{OOB} channel, which is shown in \autoref{fig:ourapproach-smartphone-flow-coordinator}. (c) Next, they verify that each group member's screen displays a green checkmark. (d) After this process, the app shows the exchanged contacts. } \label{fig:ourapproach-smartphone-flow} \Description{ A four-part composite image showing screenshots illustrating the steps of the PairSonic contact exchange process from the participant's perspective. }
elements.sent-2411-184: First, a group of users wishing to exchange contact data initiates \OurApproach on their smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-185: This brings them to the first screen, where they nominate a temporary coordinator (\autoref{fig:pairsonic-participant1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-186: The rest of the group assumes the role of participants, which brings their devices into the ready state (\autoref{fig:pairsonic-participant2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-187: The coordinator enters the total group size and confirms that all participants' smartphones are ready and nearby.\footnote{The coordinator's perspective of the \OurApproach contact exchange is depicted in \autoref{fig:ourapproach-smartphone-flow-coordinator (appendix).}}
elements.sent-2411-188: Following this, the coordinator's smartphone creates a temporary ad-hoc WiFi network, transmitting a short acoustic message containing the network details to other smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-189: This allows them to automatically join the network.\footnote{\OurApproach can function even if a user is already connected to a WiFi network; the user will temporarily switch to the ad-hoc network instead.}
elements.sent-2411-190: Next, the devices use this WiFi network to perform a cryptographic protocol, which is based on the \SafeSlinger protocol.
elements.sent-2411-191: Each participant's smartphone sends a nested commitment with their contact data\footnote{The exchanged data includes the cryptographic public key and, optionally, further information such as a profile picture.}
elements.sent-2411-192: The coordinator's smartphone generates a hash value from all participants' commitments and contact information, including their cryptographic public keys, and transmits this hash value over the acoustic \gls{OOB channel.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Other smartphones are monitoring this channel and will abort if they receive any other message.
elements.sent-2411-194: Upon receipt of the correct hash value, a green checkmark appears on the smartphones, prompting users to confirm that all other smartphones display the same checkmark (\autoref{fig:pairsonic-participant3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-195: Upon confirmation, the smartphones use the WiFi network to publish success nonces.
elements.sent-2411-196: These allow others to verify the commitments and, if verification is successful, display the exchanged contacts (\autoref{fig:pairsonic-participant4 ).}
elements.sent-2411-197: Conversely, if the checkmark is not confirmed, they release abort nonces, terminating the protocol for all participants.
elements.sent-2411-198: This procedure ensures that all participants receive accurate and verified contact information, including authenticated cryptographic public keys.
elements.subsection-2411-013: Automating the User Interaction With Acoustic Communication
elements.sent-2411-199: To reduce user effort during the protocol, we automated the majority of \SafeSlinger's user interaction.
elements.sent-2411-200: Instead of relying on users to act as data channels via error-prone shared device interactions, we utilize an \gls{OOB channel to verify the integrity of the exchanged contact information by directly connecting the devices, thereby minimizing the required user interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-201: We use an acoustic location-limited channel where smartphones repurpose their integrated speakers and microphones to exchange data within a short radius of about one meter.
elements.sent-2411-202: The smartphone speaker, rather than playing music, emits sound waves that encode information by varying their frequency or amplitude.
elements.sent-2411-203: This process has been used to connect two devices in related work (\autoref{sec:related-acousticcommunication ), and we extend this concept to multiple devices.}
elements.sent-2411-204: The other smartphones capture the sound waves, decode the embedded data, and use it as an additional security layer to verify the integrity of the exchanged contact information.
elements.sent-2411-205: The acoustic channel uniquely meets all our deployability requirements, with the added advantage that its physical layer is entirely customizable in software.
elements.sent-2411-206: This means the security of the exchanged data can be directly protected on the physical layer \cite{putz2020AcousticIntegrityCodes , an advantage when implementing our ad-hoc requirement without having to rely on existing security contexts such as pre-shared keys.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Another reason for choosing an acoustic \gls{OOB channel is that the acoustic data transmission only works when the users are in proximity and bring their devices close to each other, as depicted in \autoref{fig:teaser .}}
elements.sent-2411-208: The physical interaction of moving all devices close to each other matches the intuitive behavior that users would naturally like to perform to connect their devices \cite{kray2010Userdefined,chong2011How , similar to a handshake \cite{kuo2008Mind .}}
elements.sent-2411-209: In contrast, WiFi or Bluetooth also work over longer distances and even through walls, failing to ensure proximity.
elements.sent-2411-210: Furthermore, we use the acoustic \gls{OOB channel not only for verification but also to initiate the protocol, automatically connecting all participants' smartphones to the coordinator's WiFi Direct network.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Both WiFi and Bluetooth require a manual pairing process before communication can occur.
elements.sent-2411-212: We automate this process using acoustic communication, making the temporary network discovery and association as part of our group pairing protocol effortless and seamless.
elements.sent-2411-213: We implemented \OurApproach as an Android \gls{app using the Flutter framework and utilized Android's WiFi Direct API for local ad-hoc WiFi functionality.}
elements.sent-2411-214: The acoustic \gls{OOB channel was built using the ggwave library.\footnote{Data-over-sound library ggwave: \url{https://github.com/ggerganov/ggwave The physical layer uses multi-frequency \gls{FSK modulation with 96 evenly spaced frequencies ranging from \SI{1875 {\hertz to \SI{6375 {\hertz , according to ggwave's AUDIBLE\_FAST profile.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: We selected an audible frequency range to assess user perception of this form of communication.
elements.sent-2411-216: Practical applications could choose between audible or inaudible frequency ranges, based on factors such as robustness, usability, and hardware compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-217: As our implementation is a custom prototype using a novel communication stack combining acoustic communication with WiFi Direct, we conducted functionality and compatibility tests with smartphones from various manufacturers before our lab study.
elements.sent-2411-218: Our prototype worked on all tested devices supporting WiFi Direct, with a minimum required Android version 6.0 (API level 23, released in 2015): LG Nexus 5X, Huawei Nexus 6P, Google Pixel 4, Pixel 4a, Pixel 5, Pixel 6 Pro, Samsung Galaxy S20 Ultra, Galaxy S22, Oppo Reno 6, OnePlus 10 Pro, Xiaomi 11T Pro.
elements.sent-2411-219: Our design is principally also compatible with iOS, given the ubiquitous compatibility of acoustic communication.
elements.sent-2411-220: However, since iOS does not support WiFi Direct, this channel would need to be replaced with another communication layer supported on Apple devices, such as \gls{BLE or AWDL \cite{stute2018awdl .}}
elements.sent-2411-221: Our design does not inherently depend on any specific radio channel, making such a modification feasible.
elements.sent-2411-222: Our design's key differentiator is the automation of as many manual steps as possible, resulting in a group pairing protocol that is effortless for the user.
elements.sent-2411-223: Such a design allows us to verify our research hypothesis by comparing SafeSlinger with \OurApproach in a user study.
elements.figure-2411-002: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.1} \caption{Overview of the two group pairing protocols compared in our study.} \tablefontsize \begin{tabularx}{\columnwidth}{@{} l XX @{}} \tableheadline{\SafeSlinger} \tableheadline{\OurApproach} \tableheadline{User Grouping} \begin{tableitemize} \item peer-based \end{tableitemize} \begin{tableitemize} \item leader-based \end{tableitemize} \rowcolor{\altrowcolor} \tableheadline{Device Communication} \begin{tableitemize} \item Internet via central server \end{tableitemize} \begin{tableitemize} \item decentral via WiFi ad-hoc \item local acoustic communication \end{tableitemize} \tableheadline{Phase 1: Initialization} \begin{tableitemize} \item each participant counts + enters group size \item each participant compares ID with others and enters lowest ID \end{tableitemize} \begin{tableitemize} \item only leader counts + enters group size \item participants bring devices into proximity \end{tableitemize} \rowcolor{\altrowcolor} \tableheadline{Phase 2: Verification} \begin{tableitemize} \item each participant compares three 3-word phrases with others and selects matching phrase \end{tableitemize} \begin{tableitemize} \item each participant confirms whether all devices show green checkmark \end{tableitemize} \tableheadline{Phase 3: Finalization} \begin{tableitemize} \item each participant verifies and selects which contact entries to import \end{tableitemize} \begin{tableitemize} \item each participant verifies list of new contact entries \end{tableitemize} \end{tabularx} \label{tab:protocol-comparison}
elements.sent-2411-224: Whereas in SafeSlinger all participants must enter the group size in the initialization phase, in \OurApproach only one participant needs to enter the group size.
elements.sent-2411-225: This smartphone then starts broadcasting initialization information to the other participants' smartphones via the acoustic \gls{OOB channel.}
elements.sent-2411-226: The other participants only need to bring their devices close together.
elements.sent-2411-227: The second part of SafeSlinger's initialization phase, which requires all participants to coordinate to find and enter the lowest number shown on their devices, is completely automated in \OurApproach using the acoustic \gls{OOB channel.}
elements.sent-2411-228: After exchanging their contact information, the participants need to verify that no adversary interfered with the protocol.
elements.sent-2411-229: Whereas SafeSlinger requires all participants to find a matching word phrase among three options, we automate this using an acoustic verification message in \OurApproach.
elements.sent-2411-230: This reduces the information that participants need to verify manually to a single bit, which is represented by a clear green checkmark or a red warning symbol.
elements.sent-2411-231: The participants must abort the process in case not all devices show the green checkmark.
elements.sent-2411-232: In \OurApproach, the devices automatically verify a second acoustic message sent by the initiating smartphone to determine whether it matches the data received over the WiFi channel.
elements.sent-2411-233: In the final phase of the protocol, the participants must verify that the exchanged contact entries correspond to the involved participants.
elements.sent-2411-234: This step cannot be automated as it is a social decision to determine if a contact entry matches the person standing in front of them, rather than a technical one.
elements.sent-2411-235: In \SafeSlinger, each participant also has to select which contact entries to import.
elements.sent-2411-236: In \OurApproach, we defer this decision by importing all contact entries, considering that users can manually delete contact entries whenever they want.
elements.sent-2411-237: In addition to usability improvements, our design also addresses the deployability limitations of \SafeSlinger by decentralizing the protocol.
elements.sent-2411-238: Whereas \SafeSlinger requires a stable Internet connection during the pairing process, \OurApproach operates completely offline.
elements.sent-2411-239: Thus, it can function even in areas without WiFi or cellular connectivity, such as rural zones, emergency situations, or developing countries without Internet access.
elements.sent-2411-240: Our protocol does not require a third-party server because it operates in a decentralized manner, i.\,e., directly between the participants.
elements.sent-2411-241: This results in greater availability and resilience since our pairing process does not depend on external infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-242: It also offers a privacy advantage, as no metadata reaches any third party.
elements.sent-2411-243: While \OurApproach fundamentally changes both the user interaction and physical layer of SafeSlinger, it retains the same cryptographic protocol, thereby preserving the strong security benefits from SafeSlinger.
elements.sent-2411-244: We rely on SafeSlinger's cryptographic primitives, namely hierarchical multi-value commitments and Group Diffie-Hellman key agreement (for further information, see Farb et al. \cite{farb2013SafeSlinger ).}
elements.sent-2411-245: Additionally, we employ an acoustic \gls{OOB channel for location-limited verification as an extra security layer.}
elements.sent-2411-246: To answer our research questions on the usability and security of the group pairing protocols \SafeSlinger and \OurApproach, we conducted a comparative lab study featuring practical contact exchange tasks (\autoref{sec:methods-material ), followed by a quantitative questionnaire and qualitative interviews (\autoref{sec:methods-design ).}}
elements.sent-2411-247: We also discuss our study sample (\autoref{sec:methods-participants ), ethical considerations (\autoref{sec:methods-ethics ), and our analysis methodology (\autoref{sec:methods-analysis ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-248: Our goal for the study setting was to resemble a typical contact exchange scenario.
elements.sent-2411-249: We built a dummy smartphone \gls{app of a fictional social networking platform consisting of user profiles, direct messaging functionality, and public message boards.}
elements.sent-2411-250: This \gls{app was built using Flutter targeting Android smartphones and supports \gls{E2EE messaging using public-key cryptography.}}
elements.sent-2411-251: Each user has a profile consisting of a username and additional profile information such as an avatar and a profile description.
elements.sent-2411-252: Additionally, the \gls{app automatically generates public-key credentials for each user, which is used to secure the communication.}
elements.sent-2411-253: The app's main menu has a button to initiate the contact exchange process, triggering either \SafeSlinger or \OurApproach.

elements.sent-2411-254: We integrated \SafeSlinger into our \gls{app using the official Android library\footnote{SafeSlinger Android client library: \url{https://github.com/SafeSlingerProject/exchange-android by Farb et al. \cite{farb2013SafeSlinger , including their user interface.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: We only modified the look and feel to match the rest of our \gls{app .}

elements.sent-2411-257: We followed a mixed methods approach, combining usability testing within a lab study, open-ended oral interviews, and a post-test questionnaire.
elements.sent-2411-258: We performed our lab study with different groups of two to six participants, who got to exchange their contact information amongst each other in an actual group scenario.
elements.sent-2411-259: We used a within-subjects design to identify the differences between \OurApproach and the state of the art (\SafeSlinger{ ), meaning that each participant performed two rounds of tasks within the same group of participants to gain hands-on experience with both systems.}
elements.sent-2411-260: We used counterbalancing to vary the order in which the participants encountered these systems, which reduced practice and boredom effects.
elements.sent-2411-261: We decided for a within-subjects design due to greater statistical power than a between-subjects design.
elements.sent-2411-262: We successfully tested our study setup using a pilot study with six participants.
elements.sent-2411-263: We conducted our lab study in a neutral meeting room, where each group of participants met the study conductor, who was always present during each trial to verify that the participants performed the tasks and to conduct the interviews.
elements.sent-2411-264: The lab setting in a neutral meeting room with the same smartphones\footnote{We always used the same smartphone models for the experiment, in the following order depending on the group size: 3$\times$Google Pixel 4a, Google Pixel 5, Samsung Galaxy S22, Oppo Reno 6.}
elements.sent-2411-265: During the study, the participants filled in our questionnaire section by section after each experiment round.
elements.sent-2411-266: The study conductor additionally interviewed them about their impressions after the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-267: Participants first reviewed and signed our consent form, which outlined the study's aim, procedure, and privacy policy.
elements.sent-2411-268: We then explained the study's agenda, briefly introducing the problem of securely exchanging contact information and how current approaches fail for larger groups (approximately four minutes total).
elements.sent-2411-269: We used the same slide set for all groups to increase the internal validity of our study by ensuring that all participants received the same information.
elements.sent-2411-270: The goal of the briefing was to ensure that all participants had the same understanding of the systems they were about to try, reducing potential confounding effects from misunderstandings.
elements.sent-2411-271: Importantly, we did not disclose that we had designed one of the systems ourselves.
elements.sent-2411-272: We then handed each participant the questionnaire (\autoref{sec:questionnaire ) and a smartphone, and asked them to fill in their profile information within the \gls{app (name, avatar from a gallery of options, freely chosen text description; shown in \autoref{fig:app-profile ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-273: We gave each participant a choice between using their real name or a pseudonym within the \gls{app .}
elements.sent-2411-274: Upon distributing the smartphones, we conducted our experiment in two rounds (A and B) corresponding to both group pairing systems.
elements.sent-2411-275: Each round was structured identically, but we alternated the order of \SafeSlinger and \OurApproach between each group to neutralize order effects.

elements.sent-2411-276: Each round started with a warm-up task, where we asked the group to try the contact exchange system on their own without further guidance except the prompts within the \gls{app .}
elements.sent-2411-277: The study conductor noted down potential problems in the study protocol and clarified the process with the group after they successfully exchanged their contact information.
elements.sent-2411-278: We then asked the group to use the system a second time, but remotely and unbeknownst to the participants we manipulated the experiment in such a way as to simulate an active attack on the pairing process.
elements.sent-2411-279: This resulted in the protocol failing on one of the smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-280: The study conductor observed the group reacting to this situation and asked them why they thought the process failed.
elements.sent-2411-281: We opted to simulate an attack because this provides a controllable and reproducible method to ensure all participants have a consistent experience.
elements.sent-2411-282: By simulating rather than actively disrupting the wireless communication, we also avoided technical risks to nearby networks.
elements.sent-2411-283: Our simulated attack mirrors the maximum capabilities of our theoretical adversary, who can remotely control smartphone radio communication undetected (\autoref{sec:adversary-model ).}
elements.sent-2411-284: Incorporating this attack into our study helped us evaluate the usability of \OurApproach, especially in adversarial scenarios our system aims to counter.
elements.sent-2411-285: After explaining that this protocol failure could indicate a security problem, we asked the group to use the system a third time.
elements.sent-2411-286: We remotely disabled the attack simulation and told them that this time there would be no attack.
elements.sent-2411-287: At the same time, we measured the time it took the group to perform this third task from the beginning till the end, to get an assessment of how fast the system works after some practice.
elements.sent-2411-288: After the first round, we collected all smartphones, switched to the other group pairing protocol, and conducted the second round likewise.
elements.sent-2411-289: Before concluding the study, we thanked the participants for their time.
elements.sent-2411-290: In total, each of our participants spent 60 minutes to take part in our study.
elements.sent-2411-291: After each of the two rounds, participants filled in the respective sections of the questionnaire to reflect on their experiences during the experiment tasks.
elements.sent-2411-292: Once both rounds were completed, they filled in sections about their preference of both system, about their usage patterns of different social or collaborative tools, and demographic questions at the end.
elements.sent-2411-293: Our corresponding questionnaire, presented in \autoref{sec:questionnaire , was used to collect the following variables:.}
elements.sent-2411-294: The dependent variables captured our participants post-test assessment of both \OurApproach and \SafeSlinger.
elements.sent-2411-295: For each system, we used the \gls{SUS \cite{brooke1996sus to answer RQ2 on usability.}}
elements.sent-2411-296: Because efficiency is one aspect of usability \cite{rubin2008Handbook , the study conductor also measured the time it took the groups to complete the protocol during the third task.}
elements.sent-2411-297: To answer RQ3, we asked an additional question about the perceived security of the systems, using a five-level Likert item.
elements.sent-2411-298: To answer RQ1, this binary variable captures the participants' preference: either they preferred \OurApproach or \SafeSlinger.
elements.sent-2411-300: To measure our participants' use of collaborative platforms and tools, we provided a list of nine distinct types of digital communication groups.
elements.sent-2411-301: For each type, we asked the participants to state whether they actively took part in them, the average number of participants in such groups, and if security was important to them in these contexts.
elements.sent-2411-302: Participants could also provide information about additional group types using free text fields.
elements.sent-2411-303: Additionally, we requested them to estimate the total number of digital communication groups they overall participate in.
elements.sent-2411-304: We used two control variables: participants' prior experience with smartphones and their \gls{ATI \cite{franke2019affinity .}}
elements.sent-2411-305: Both variables help to assess the background of our sample and to explore potential correlations between technology affinity and the usability or security of both systems \cite{chong2013How .}
elements.sent-2411-306: As a standardized measure, the \gls{ATI also improves the utility of our dataset for future research on usable security.}
elements.sent-2411-307: We collected the gender, age, education, field of study/work, and student status of our participants as demographic variables.
elements.sent-2411-308: At four stages during the study, we interviewed our participants with open-ended qualitative questions to better understand their behavior and reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-309: All participants agreed to be recorded in our consent form.
elements.sent-2411-310: The study conductor also asked follow-up questions to learn more about the intricacies of their reasoning, but always asked the same set of starting questions for each group, in accordance with our research questions.

elements.subsection-2411-026: Which Method Do Users Prefer Overall?
elements.sent-2411-393: During the interviews, our participants' comments aligned with their preference for \OurApproach overall.
elements.sent-2411-394: They specifically highlighted two key advantages: better usability and scalability: \studyquote{If you have used [\OurApproach{] once or twice, then it's also just much, much more convenient than [\SafeSlinger{] .}}}
elements.sent-2411-395: I mean, you open the app, say \guillemotright here, you're the person who sets this up\guillemotleft, hold the phones together, done.
elements.sent-2411-396: Regarding security, the qualitative results were mixed, supporting the results of our quantitative analysis (\autoref{sec:quantitative-security ).}
elements.sent-2411-397: Some participants expressed that \SafeSlinger's additional user interaction and complexity instilled a sense of enhanced security compared to the simpler \OurApproach.
elements.sent-2411-398: Conversely, other participants favored the security of \OurApproach, as it did not solely rely on potentially error-prone user interaction but also incorporated acoustic verification as an additional layer of security.
elements.sent-2411-399: Most participants raised concerns about the Internet requirement of \SafeSlinger, citing instances where they lacked cellular connectivity or access to public WiFi.
elements.sent-2411-400: They worried that this could limit the situations in which they can exchange their contact data.
elements.sent-2411-401: Additionally, a few participants expressed privacy concerns since the contact data was transmitted over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-402: Despite \SafeSlinger being designed to safeguard the confidentiality of exchanged information from third parties \cite{farb2013SafeSlinger , the fact that the protocol relied on Internet connectivity was enough to make some participants worry about their data.}
elements.sent-2411-403: In contrast, the decentralized nature of \OurApproach emerged as a distinct privacy and availability advantage, as no third parties are required to facilitate the protocol.
elements.subsection-2411-027: Which Initialization Step Do Users Prefer?
elements.sent-2411-404: The initialization step of \SafeSlinger and \OurApproach temporarily associates the group members' devices to exchange contact information and cryptographic public keys.

elements.sent-2411-406: In \SafeSlinger, a peer-based approach is used, requiring each group member to input the group size and determine the lowest identification number among all members.
elements.sent-2411-407: On the other hand, \OurApproach utilizes a coordinator-based approach, where only one group member needs to input the group size, and the rest of the initialization is automated through acoustic communication.
elements.sent-2411-408: It is important to note that the role of the group leader in \OurApproach does not confer any special privileges and is only relevant to the contact exchange without implications for subsequent communication.
